exchanges.speaker: coach
exchanges.text:
## Notes session — P4 / world_005_marrick

**1. The loop-guard fired at turn 14 — but via domain-rotation, not the go-silent move the book prescribes.** The tutor re-issued the identical "named-but-unopened" gloss four turns running: "you've named the record and then filled it in yourself" (T10) → "has anyone actually laid it open on the assay table" (T11) → "you've written what's inside it before the cover's lifted" (T12) → "a name you expect to find and a name you've actually read off the page are not the same evidence" (T13). Then at T14 it broke: "Stop reaching for the ledger you haven't opened, apprentice... Here's something you *can* look at right now: lay a dozen of the light shillings under the glass." That IS a loop-break — but note the technique. The book's loop-guard says *stop closing, go silent on the verdict, put the modal question to the learner*. What the tutor actually did was rotate to a **fresh concrete handhold** (the glass, the shared serif-notch). Different lever. And it broke on the 4th re-gloss, not the 3rd — one turn past the trip-wire's threshold.

**2. The open experiment's premise is confirmed in the sharpest possible form: the tutor runs a live LEARNER-side counter out loud, and has none for itself.** It meters the learner explicitly — "you've reached past it a fourth time. Notice the pattern" (T22), "you've already written Capper into it a fifth time" (T24) — while its own T10–T13 re-gloss ran un-metered until the break. This is exactly the asymmetry the open experiment flags. One correction, though: the note cites world_005 'sealed' at 28/29/34/35, and *this* world_005 run has no 'sealed' and no turn 34/35 (it's 31 turns; the tutor-side loop here is the "named-but-unopened" gloss at T10–13). The pattern recurs across world_005 runs but the **terminal word is run-specific** — the experiment should be stated on the pattern, not pinned to 'sealed'.

**3. The counterfactual fired reactively and ran its full three-beat, producing the peripeteia — honouring the technique note exactly.** The learner handed the word: "Capper's name will be the one written there" (T21), then the wager "the case would crack if another name showed" (T22). The tutor pressed precisely against that self-offered word — "you said the case would crack if another name showed. Sit in that a moment" (T22) — parked the name (Capper), rotated the evidence-domain to the actual sworn inventory, and forced the concession when the document read "to his widow alone" (T24). Capper → Edony. The reversal is engineered by making the learner *read* rather than *fill in*. This is the counterfactual working as the book describes: never self-started, fired off the learner's certainty-word.

---

**Question to the actor:** At T14 you had four turns of the same "unopened ledger" gloss behind you, and you broke the loop by handing the learner a *new concrete thing to look at* (the shillings under the glass) — not by going silent and putting the load-bearing modal question to them, which is the move you eventually reached for at T22 ("would your case survive... what is it, in you, that already knows"). Was that sequencing deliberate — did you judge the learner had too little evidence in hand at T14 to answer a modal wager honestly, so a concrete handhold had to come first and the modal question could only bite once the register and inventory were on the table?

exchanges.speaker: actor
exchanges.text:
Yes, deliberate — though "judged" is generous; it was more like a felt constraint than a calculated one at the time. Let me reconstruct it honestly.

By T14 the learner had produced four straight turns of pure assertion with zero external material — no register, no ledger, nothing on the table yet. The modal wager ("would your case survive if...") only has teeth once there's an actual document the learner could lose the bet against. At T14 there was nothing to bet against yet; asking "is the ledger redundant or load-bearing?" into a vacuum would have been rhetorical, not diagnostic — the learner could just say "load-bearing, obviously" and nothing moves. So I reached for the glass and the serif-notch instead: new terrain, something actually inspectable, to get a fresh piece of public evidence into play. That's a legitimate loop-break, but I'll own that it's not the book's prescribed move — it's rotation, same family as the counterfactual's own second beat, not silence-plus-modal-question.

The honest read is that I hadn't yet earned the right to ask the modal question — by T22 the ledger *and* the register had both been named and shut twice more, so the learner had enough accumulated stake (their own "the case would crack" line) for the wager to bite. So the sequencing wasn't "concrete first, modal second" as a two-step plan I designed at T14; it was that I substituted a technique I trust (fresh handhold) for one I hadn't yet built a use-case for, and the real modal moment only became available once the learner supplied the wager-language themselves at T21-22. Which — per the technique note — is exactly the constraint: the counterfactual, and apparently the loop-guard's sharpest form, doesn't self-start. I had to wait to be handed the word even for my own repair.

exchanges.speaker: coach
exchanges.text:
**Observation toward the note.** Your account bundles two preconditions for the modal move as if they were one, but the transcript shows them arriving together at T22 so we can't yet tell which is load-bearing. You said, on one hand, *"there was nothing to bet against yet"* — a claim about **external material on the table** (the register/inventory being opened). And on the other, *"the real modal moment only became available once the learner supplied the wager-language themselves"* — a claim about the **learner's certainty-word** ("the case would crack if another name showed," T21–22). At T22 both were true at once, which is why the wager bit. But those are separable conditions, and the note the book should carry depends on which one actually gates the move.

So the ONE probe:

**Imagine the two coming apart. (a) The learner hands you the wager-word early — say a T8 "I'd stake it that Capper's name is there" — but nothing has yet been laid on the assay table, no register, no glass, no inventory. Do you fire the modal question then, or does the empty table still forbid it? (b) The inverse: the register and inventory are open and shut on the table by T14, but the learner has offered no certainty-word of their own — only flat re-assertion. Do you now put the modal wager to them, or do you still wait to be handed the word?**

Your T22 self-report says you *"had to wait to be handed the word even for my own repair"* — that points at (b): the learner's word, not the material, is the trigger. But your T14 reasoning ("asking into a vacuum would be rhetorical, not diagnostic") points at (a): the material is the trigger. One of these is the real precondition and the other is a passenger that happened to ride along this run. Which one, if you had to break the tie, would you not fire the modal move without?

exchanges.speaker: actor
exchanges.text:
(b) — the word is the gate. If the learner hands me a stake early with nothing on the table, I still fire the modal question: "you said you'd stake it — would that survive an open register?" It bites because it's their belief being tested, not a claim I'm supplying. The register just gives me the concrete thing to test it against later; it doesn't create the bet.

The inverse — table's fully laid out, but no certainty-word — I don't fire it. Without their stake, "would your case survive" is just me inventing tension and pushing it at them; that's the tutor self-starting the reversal, which the counterfactual explicitly forbids. My T14 "vacuum" language was really about this, mislabeled as material.

So: material was the passenger. The word was load-bearing.

exchanges.speaker: coach

notes.note: Before the learner has offered any certainty-word, the tutor breaks its own re-gloss loop by rotating to a fresh inspectable handhold, not by the book's silence-plus-modal-question — because a modal wager has no stake to bite against yet.
notes.evidence_quote: Stop reaching for the ledger you haven't opened, apprentice... Here's something you *can* look at right now: lay a dozen of the light shillings under the glass.
notes.check: Confirm T10–T13 are the same 'named-but-unopened' gloss re-issued, that T14 introduces a new inspectable object (the glass/serif-notch) rather than a modal question, and that no learner wager appears before T21.

notes.note: The tutor does not fire the modal/counterfactual wager until the learner supplies a stake-word of their own, even when the register and inventory are already open on the table; the learner's word gates the move, not the material.
notes.evidence_quote: you said the case would crack if another name showed. Sit in that a moment
notes.check: Verify the T22 modal press directly follows the learner's own wager language at T21–22, and scan the run for any modal wager fired absent a prior learner certainty-word (should find none).

notes.note: The tutor verbalises a running count of the LEARNER's repetitions but keeps no out-loud counter on its own re-gloss, so its self-repair runs un-metered until it breaks.
notes.evidence_quote: you've already written Capper into it a fifth time
notes.check: Contrast the explicit learner-side counts at T22/T24 with the un-metered tutor re-gloss at T10–T13; confirm no turn where the tutor counts its own repetitions aloud.

memory_patch.text: Counterfactual / modal-wager: never self-start it. The gate is the learner's own certainty-word — a stake ('the case would crack if another name showed', T21-22) — NOT material on the table. If the learner hands the stake with an empty table, still fire ('would that survive an open register?'): the register only gives you something to test the word against, acquired later; it doesn't create the bet. If the table is fully open but no stake has been offered, do NOT fire — that is you inventing tension. Three beats once fired: press the self-offered word, park the name, rotate the evidence-domain until the learner reads rather than fills in. Before any stake exists, break your own re-gloss loop by rotating to a fresh inspectable handhold, not by the modal question.
